articles.headline: Russian Soldiers Describe Executions of Troops Who Refuse Orders
articles.subheadline: Two men who fled Russia's military describe witnessing commanders shoot soldiers at point-blank range on the front lines in Ukraine
articles.body:
Russian soldiers have come forward with firsthand accounts of commanders executing their own troops for refusing to fight, in testimony that offers a stark window into the brutality and discipline crises plaguing Moscow's war effort in Ukraine.

In a BBC documentary titled "The Zero Line: Inside Russia's War," two men speaking from an undisclosed location outside Russia described witnessing what is known in Russian military slang as "zeroing" — the summary killing of soldiers who refuse orders or attempt to flee the front lines. Ilya, a 35-year-old former special needs teacher who was drafted into the army, told filmmakers he personally witnessed a commander shoot four soldiers at point-blank range after they fled their positions and refused to return. The practice, both men said, serves a dual purpose: punishing disobedience and terrorizing others who might consider doing the same.

A second witness, identified only as Dima, described the killings with chilling matter-of-factness. "I see it — just two metres, three metres. Just murders, just click, clack, bang. It's not a drama, it's not a movie, it's a real life," he said, adding that commanders killing their own personnel had become "a normal thing." Both men are currently on the run, having themselves abandoned the Russian military.

The accounts align with a growing body of reporting suggesting severe morale and discipline problems within Russia's armed forces more than three years into the full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Previous investigations by media outlets and Ukrainian intelligence have pointed to instances of Russian troops being threatened or killed by their own side for retreating, though independent verification of individual incidents remains difficult.

The Russian government, responding to the BBC's reporting, said its armed forces "operate with utmost restraint" and treat personnel "with maximum care." Moscow added that "information regarding alleged violations and crimes is duly investigated" but said it was "unable to independently verify the accuracy or authenticity" of the claims presented. The Kremlin has consistently denied systemic abuses within its military ranks, even as evidence of harsh conditions, forced mobilization, and the extensive use of convict recruits has mounted throughout the conflict.

articles.headline: New Method Updates Reservoir Simulations Hundreds of Times Faster
articles.subheadline: Researchers show how to adapt data-driven surrogate models for carbon storage without costly retraining
articles.body:
A pair of researchers has developed a technique that could dramatically accelerate computational modeling for geological carbon storage projects, enabling real-time optimization of subsurface simulations that currently require expensive retraining whenever reservoir conditions change.

Dimitrios Voulanas and Eduardo Gildin, in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server, describe a lightweight method for updating Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) operators — a popular data-driven framework for reducing the computational burden of multiphase flow simulations. The central problem they address is a practical one: DMD models are trained on a specific set of reservoir properties, such as rock permeability and well placement. When those properties change, as they inevitably do during optimization or uncertainty analysis, the conventional approach requires generating entirely new simulation snapshots and retraining the surrogate model from scratch, negating much of the speed advantage that made the approach attractive in the first place.

The proposed solution introduces two complementary update strategies that avoid new simulation data altogether. For uniform changes in permeability across a reservoir domain, the method adjusts the model's internal dynamics and control response to match the altered flow timescale. When permeability varies spatially — a more realistic scenario reflecting the heterogeneity of real geological formations — the approach instead modifies the spatial representation, weighting high-permeability zones more heavily in the model's reduced basis. According to the authors' numerical experiments, the updated models reproduce plume migration patterns and pressure build-up to within three percent of a freshly trained surrogate, while executing hundreds of times faster than full retraining.

The implications extend directly to the growing field of carbon capture and storage (CCS), where operators must repeatedly evaluate subsurface scenarios to optimize injection strategies, assess storage security, and quantify uncertainty. High-fidelity multiphase simulators that model CO₂ migration through porous rock remain prohibitively expensive for the iterative workflows demanded by real-world decision-making. Surrogate models built on DMD have emerged as a promising middle ground, but their brittleness to parameter changes has limited practical deployment.

By decoupling the surrogate model from the need for fresh training data each time conditions shift, the new framework could enable rapid what-if analyses during carbon storage planning — a capability that regulators and project developers increasingly require as CCS scales from pilot demonstrations to industrial-scale operations. The work, which spans computational physics, engineering, and optimization theory, was submitted on November 27, 2025.

articles.headline: Waterloo Scientists Program Bacteria to Devour Tumors From Within
articles.subheadline: Engineered microbes use quorum sensing to activate oxygen tolerance only after colonizing a tumor's core
articles.body:
Researchers at the University of Waterloo have engineered a soil bacterium to infiltrate solid tumors, consume cancerous tissue from the inside, and survive in conditions that would normally kill it — all triggered by a built-in biological timer that activates only when the microbes reach critical mass.

The approach centers on Clostridium sporogenes, an anaerobic bacterium that thrives exclusively in oxygen-free environments. Because the cores of many solid tumors are composed of dead cells and devoid of oxygen, they provide ideal conditions for the microbe to germinate, feed on available nutrients, and multiply. "Bacteria spores enter the tumor, finding an environment where there are lots of nutrients and no oxygen, which this organism prefers, and so it starts eating those nutrients and growing in size," said Marc Aucoin, a chemical engineering professor at Waterloo. The bacterium essentially colonizes the tumor's interior and begins destroying it.

The central engineering challenge has been what happens at the tumor's periphery. As the bacteria expand outward from the oxygen-starved core, they encounter trace amounts of oxygen and begin to die before they can eliminate the cancer entirely. To solve this, the Waterloo team inserted a gene from a related, more oxygen-tolerant bacterium into C. sporogenes, enabling the engineered microbes to survive longer near the tumor's oxygenated edges. But introducing oxygen tolerance raised a serious safety concern: if the trait activated prematurely, the bacteria could proliferate in the bloodstream or healthy tissue.

The solution came from quorum sensing, a natural bacterial communication mechanism in which microbes release chemical signals that intensify as their population grows. The team wired the oxygen-tolerance gene to activate only after the bacterial colony inside a tumor reaches a sufficient density — ensuring the survival upgrade switches on precisely where and when it is needed. Brian Ingalls, a professor of applied mathematics at Waterloo, likened the design to an electrical circuit built from DNA. "Each piece has its job. When assembled correctly, they form a system that works in a predictable way," he said. The quorum-sensing system was validated in experiments where bacteria were programmed to produce green fluorescent protein at the intended activation threshold, as detailed in a paper published in ACS Synthetic Biology.

The next milestone is combining both the oxygen-tolerance gene and the quorum-sensing control circuit into a single organism and testing it against tumors in pre-clinical trials. The Waterloo team is collaborating with CREM Co Labs, a Toronto-based company co-founded by PhD student Bahram Zargar, who initiated the research alongside Ingalls and retired Waterloo professor Pu Chen. Former doctoral student Sara Sadr played a leading role in advancing the work. If the integrated system performs as designed in animal models, it could open a fundamentally new front in oncology — one in which programmable living organisms replace or complement conventional therapies to attack solid tumors that have long resisted treatment.

articles.headline: Anthropic Says Chinese AI Labs Used 24,000 Fake Accounts to Extract Claude's Capabilities
articles.subheadline: DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax allegedly generated 16 million exchanges in massive distillation campaign targeting reasoning and coding skills
articles.body:
Anthropic has accused three major Chinese artificial intelligence companies of systematically extracting capabilities from its Claude AI model through a large-scale distillation operation involving more than 24,000 fraudulent accounts and 16 million exchanges, intensifying the geopolitical battle over AI supremacy between the United States and China.

The San Francisco-based AI company identified DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax as the perpetrators, alleging each lab targeted Claude's most advanced features — agentic reasoning, tool use, and coding — using distillation, a technique in which one AI model's outputs are used to train another. While distillation is a legitimate method when applied to a company's own models, using it to replicate a competitor's capabilities amounts to what Anthropic characterizes as intellectual theft on an industrial scale.

The three firms pursued distinct strategies, according to Anthropic's findings. DeepSeek, which stunned the industry a year ago with its R1 reasoning model, accounted for roughly 150,000 exchanges focused on foundational logic and alignment — particularly around generating censorship-safe responses to policy-sensitive queries. Moonshot AI was far more prolific, generating 3.4 million exchanges targeting agentic reasoning, coding, and computer vision capabilities ahead of its recent Kimi K2.5 open-source model release. MiniMax conducted the largest operation at 13 million exchanges focused on agentic coding and tool orchestration, with Anthropic observing the company redirect nearly half its traffic to extract capabilities from the newest Claude model at launch.

The allegations land at a politically charged moment. The Trump administration last month permitted U.S. companies including Nvidia to resume exporting advanced AI chips such as the H200 to China, a move critics argue undermines efforts to constrain Beijing's AI development. Anthropic explicitly linked the distillation campaign to the export control debate, arguing that such attacks "require access to advanced chips" and therefore reinforce the case for maintaining restrictions. Dmitri Alperovitch, co-founder of CrowdStrike and chairman of the Silverado Policy Accelerator, said the revelations confirm what many suspected. "Part of the reason for the rapid progress of Chinese AI models has been theft via distillation of U.S. frontier models. Now we know this for a fact," he said.

Beyond competitive concerns, Anthropic warned of national security implications, noting that distilled models are unlikely to retain safety guardrails designed to prevent misuse in bioweapons development, offensive cyber operations, and disinformation campaigns. The company called for a coordinated response across the AI industry, cloud providers, and policymakers to counter what it described as an ongoing and escalating threat. DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax have not publicly responded to the accusations.

articles.headline: JPMorgan's Dimon Warns of Pre-2008 Parallels as Rivals Chase Risk
articles.subheadline: The longest-serving major bank CEO says competitive pressure is driving lenders to repeat dangerous mistakes
articles.body:
Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JPMorgan Chase & Co., has drawn a pointed comparison between the current competitive landscape in banking and the reckless lending environment that preceded the 2008 financial crisis, warning that rivals are engaging in what he characterized as "dumb things" in their pursuit of market share.

The remarks, reported by Bloomberg on February 24, underscore growing unease among some senior figures in finance about the intensity of competition across lending, trading, and investment banking. Dimon — the longest-tenured CEO among the major Wall Street banks, having led JPMorgan since 2005 — has the distinction of having steered his institution through the last financial crisis in stronger shape than most peers. His willingness to invoke that era carries particular weight on Wall Street, where memories of the mortgage meltdown and its cascading consequences still shape risk management culture.

While Dimon did not publicly detail which specific practices or competitors concerned him, the thrust of his warning echoed a familiar pattern: when economic conditions appear benign and capital is abundant, banks loosen underwriting standards and chase volume to maintain revenue growth. In the years leading up to 2008, that dynamic fueled an explosion of subprime mortgage lending, complex securitizations, and excessive leverage that ultimately triggered a global financial crisis. The implication of Dimon's comments is that a similar erosion of discipline may be underway, even if the specific instruments and sectors involved differ from the last cycle.

The warning comes at a time when JPMorgan, the largest U.S. bank by assets, has been posting strong results and expanding aggressively in areas including consumer banking and artificial intelligence. Dimon has frequently used his platform to comment on macroeconomic risks, geopolitical tensions, and regulatory policy, often positioning JPMorgan as a more disciplined operator than its competitors. Critics sometimes view such pronouncements as strategic messaging designed to burnish that image, but the bank's track record during the 2008 crisis lends the CEO's cautionary notes an outsized audience.

For regulators and market participants, the remarks are likely to add to an ongoing debate about whether current capital buffers and supervisory frameworks are sufficient to contain the risks building in the system. With credit markets buoyant and deal activity accelerating, Dimon's invocation of pre-crisis parallels serves as a stark reminder that the seeds of financial instability are most often planted during periods of confidence.

articles.headline: Jane Street Sued Over Alleged Insider Trading in Terra's $40B Collapse
articles.subheadline: Terraform Labs' bankruptcy administrator claims the trading giant used nonpublic information to front-run trades that triggered the 2022 crash
articles.body:
The administrator overseeing the wind-down of Terraform Labs has filed a lawsuit against Jane Street, one of Wall Street's most powerful high-frequency trading firms, alleging that it exploited insider information to front-run trades during the catastrophic collapse of the TerraUSD stablecoin in May 2022 — a crisis that erased roughly $40 billion in market value within a single week.

Todd Snyder, the court-appointed administrator unwinding Do Kwon's bankrupt crypto venture, filed the suit against Jane Street, its co-founder Robert Granieri, and employees Bryce Pratt and Michael Huang, according to reporting by the Wall Street Journal. The complaint centers on events beginning May 7, 2022, when Terraform quietly withdrew 150 million TerraUSD from Curve3pool, a decentralized stablecoin trading platform. Within 10 minutes of that undisclosed withdrawal, a wallet linked to Jane Street allegedly pulled an additional 85 million TerraUSD from the same pool — before any public announcement had been made. The combined withdrawals are alleged to have set off the panic that broke TerraUSD's dollar peg and sent the Luna token spiraling to zero.

"Jane Street abused market relationships to rig the market in its favor during one of the most consequential events in crypto history," Snyder said in a statement, adding that he would "pursue all avenues supported by the facts and the law" on behalf of injured creditors. The lawsuit also points to communications from May 9, 2022, in which Jane Street's Pratt allegedly contacted Kwon's team via group chat to offer purchases of bitcoin or Luna as the stablecoin began to slip, with Kwon responding that Jump Trading's co-founder Bill DiSomma should have informed them earlier about Terraform's fundraising efforts.

Jane Street has forcefully rejected the allegations. A spokesman for the firm called the lawsuit a "desperate" and "transparent attempt to extract money," arguing that the losses suffered by Terra and Luna holders were the direct result of "a multibillion-dollar fraud perpetrated by the management of Terraform Labs." The firm pledged to defend vigorously against what it characterized as "baseless, opportunistic claims."

The lawsuit adds a new legal dimension to one of crypto's most devastating episodes. Terraform Labs, the Singapore-based blockchain company founded in 2018 by Kwon and Daniel Shin, filed for bankruptcy in January 2024. Kwon himself pleaded guilty to two criminal counts and was sentenced to 15 years in prison in August 2025. The Terra implosion triggered a cascade of failures across the crypto industry, toppling lending platforms and hedge funds with exposure to the project. If Snyder's allegations hold up in court, they could establish that the damage was not solely the product of Terraform's own fraudulent design but was materially worsened by sophisticated market participants trading on privileged information.

articles.headline: Amundi Takes Long Position on Japanese Bonds for First Time in Decades
articles.subheadline: Europe's largest asset manager joins growing cohort of foreign investors betting on Japanese government debt
articles.body:
Amundi, Europe's largest asset manager with more than €2 trillion in assets under management, has taken a long position on Japanese government bonds for the first time in roughly three decades, signaling a significant shift in institutional sentiment toward the world's second-largest sovereign debt market.

The move places Amundi alongside a widening group of foreign investors adopting a bullish long-term outlook on Japanese government bonds, or JGBs, a market that has long been considered inhospitable to international buyers. For years, Japan's ultra-low and even negative interest rate environment made its sovereign debt unattractive to yield-seeking global fund managers. The so-called "widowmaker" trade — betting against JGBs — became legendary on Wall Street for punishing those who wagered that Japanese bond prices would eventually fall as yields rose.

The landscape has shifted meaningfully since the Bank of Japan began unwinding its decades-long experiment with ultra-loose monetary policy. As the BOJ has gradually raised interest rates and stepped back from its aggressive yield curve control program, Japanese bond yields have climbed to levels not seen in years, making JGBs a more viable proposition for global fixed-income portfolios. Rising yields translate to higher coupon income and, for those who time their entry well, the potential for capital appreciation if economic conditions eventually warrant a policy reversal.

Amundi's decision carries particular weight given its scale and influence across European institutional markets. The Paris-headquartered firm's move could encourage other large allocators to reconsider their own underweight positions in Japanese debt. Foreign ownership of JGBs has historically been modest compared with domestic holdings dominated by Japanese banks, insurance companies, and the Bank of Japan itself, but a sustained inflow of international capital could reshape the market's dynamics.

The timing also reflects broader macroeconomic crosscurrents. With sovereign bond markets in the United States and Europe facing their own fiscal and inflationary uncertainties, some global investors are looking to diversify their fixed-income exposure. Japan, with its relatively stable political environment and deep capital markets, offers an alternative that appears increasingly attractive after years of being overlooked by all but the most specialized international bond managers.

articles.headline: Indian IT Stocks Extend Selloff as AI Disruption Fears Intensify
articles.subheadline: Citrini Research report amplifies concerns that AI could erode demand for India's $250 billion outsourcing industry
articles.body:
Indian information technology stocks deepened their slide on Monday, with the sector facing its steepest selloff in months as a widely circulated report from Citrini Research stoked fears that artificial intelligence is poised to fundamentally disrupt the country's outsourcing-dependent software services industry.

The downturn, which tracked a broader technology selloff in U.S. markets, hit India's largest IT exporters hard. Companies such as Tata Consultancy Services, Infosys, Wipro, and HCL Technologies — which collectively employ millions of workers and form the backbone of India's services export economy — saw significant declines as investors reassessed the sector's long-term growth trajectory in an era of rapidly advancing AI capabilities. The Citrini Research report specifically flagged Indian IT firms as among the most vulnerable to displacement as enterprises increasingly turn to AI-powered tools for tasks traditionally outsourced to human software engineers and service providers.

The concerns are not entirely new, but they have gained urgency as large language models and AI coding assistants demonstrate growing proficiency in software development, testing, and maintenance — core revenue streams for Indian IT companies. The sector, which generates roughly $250 billion in annual revenue and accounts for a substantial share of India's GDP, has long positioned itself as a cost-effective alternative for global enterprises seeking to manage their technology operations. That value proposition faces a direct challenge if AI tools can perform similar work faster and at lower cost.

Indian IT executives have pushed back against the most dire predictions, arguing that their firms are actively integrating AI into their own service offerings and that the technology will augment rather than replace human workers. Several major companies have announced significant investments in AI training and reskilling programs. However, investors appear unconvinced that these efforts will fully offset the potential revenue erosion, particularly as hyperscale cloud providers and AI startups compete to automate enterprise software workflows.

The selloff underscores a broader market reckoning with AI's implications for labor-intensive industries. While U.S. technology firms have largely benefited from the AI boom as builders of the underlying infrastructure, companies positioned as service providers face the opposite calculus — the very technology driving Silicon Valley's gains threatens to compress margins and shrink addressable markets for outsourcing giants. For India, where the IT sector is a critical engine of middle-class employment and foreign exchange earnings, the stakes extend well beyond stock prices.

articles.headline: Lord Mandelson Arrested Over Alleged Leak of Sensitive Info to Epstein
articles.subheadline: Former UK ambassador to the US released on bail after Metropolitan Police detained him on misconduct charges
articles.body:
Lord Peter Mandelson, the veteran Labour figure and former British ambassador to the United States, was arrested Monday on suspicion of misconduct in public office and released on bail in the early hours of Tuesday, according to the Metropolitan Police.

The 72-year-old was detained at his Camden home in north London by officers from the Met's central specialist crime division and taken to a police station for questioning. BBC cameras captured him returning home at approximately 2:00 a.m. GMT. Police also executed search warrants at two addresses in Wiltshire and Camden as part of the investigation, which was launched earlier this month over allegations that Mandelson passed market-sensitive government information to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein while serving as a cabinet minister.

The allegations stem from a cache of documents released last month by the U.S. Department of Justice, which included emails between Mandelson and Epstein dating to 2009 and 2010. One email appeared to show Mandelson sharing an internal assessment from an adviser to then-Prime Minister Gordon Brown regarding policy measures including an "asset sales plan." Other communications appeared to reference a tax on bankers' bonuses and to confirm an imminent eurozone bailout package the day before it was publicly announced. While Mandelson has not commented publicly on the Epstein files in recent weeks, sources close to him say he maintains he did not act criminally and was not motivated by financial gain.

Mandelson was appointed British ambassador to Washington in February 2025 but was dismissed in September after Downing Street said new information about the depth of his relationship with Epstein had surfaced. The arrest has intensified political pressure on Prime Minister Keir Starmer, who appointed Mandelson to the diplomatic post. Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch called the arrest "the defining moment" of Starmer's premiership, saying the image of the prime minister's appointee being led away by police would endure for years. Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson said the government remains committed to releasing documents related to Mandelson's appointment but must avoid jeopardizing the live police investigation.

The family of the late Virginia Giuffre, who accused Prince Andrew of sexual abuse tied to Epstein, praised British authorities for "taking meaningful action" and contrasted it with what they called "continued inaction in the United States." U.S. lawmakers have separately urged Mandelson to testify before Congress as part of their own Epstein investigation. Consultations between the Metropolitan Police and the Crown Prosecution Service are ongoing, and further developments are expected as investigators review the seized materials.

articles.headline: Panama Seizes Canal Ports After Court Voids CK Hutchison Concession
articles.subheadline: President Mulino orders temporary occupation of two strategic ports, deepening a U.S.-China proxy battle in Latin America
articles.body:
Panama's government moved to take control of two key ports along the Panama Canal on Monday after the country's top court struck down the concession held by a unit of Hong Kong-based CK Hutchison Holdings Ltd., dramatically escalating a geopolitical dispute that has positioned the Central American nation at the center of U.S.-China rivalry.

President José Raul Mulino announced the temporary occupation of both ports in a televised address, directing the National Maritime Authority to assume administration and operations immediately. The order is designed to ensure "uninterrupted, safe and efficient operation" of the facilities, which sit at one of the world's most critical shipping chokepoints. Mulino emphasized that the seizure covers movable equipment, including cranes, and does not constitute a permanent forfeiture of ownership rights — the state will return property once the underlying legal dispute is resolved or pay compensation if the assets are transferred to a new operator.

The court ruling that triggered the takeover invalidated CK Hutchison's concession to operate the ports, though full details of the legal reasoning have not yet been made public. CK Hutchison, the conglomerate controlled by the family of billionaire Li Ka-shing, has operated ports at both the Pacific and Atlantic entrances to the canal for decades under long-term agreements with the Panamanian government. The company had previously faced intense pressure from Washington, which under former President Donald Trump openly challenged Chinese-linked companies' presence near the strategically vital waterway.

The Panama Canal handles roughly five percent of global maritime trade, and the ports flanking it are essential nodes in supply chains connecting Asia, the Americas, and Europe. Washington has long viewed Chinese commercial interests in the canal zone as a national security concern, and the dispute has become emblematic of broader competition between the United States and China for influence across Latin America. Beijing has denied any strategic intent behind its companies' commercial operations in the region.

For CK Hutchison, which operates one of the world's largest port networks spanning dozens of countries, the loss of the Panamanian concession represents both a significant commercial setback and a signal that geopolitical pressures can override decades-old business arrangements. The company has not yet publicly responded to the court's ruling or the government's occupation order. Analysts will be watching closely to see whether Panama moves to rebid the concession — and whether any new operator carries the implicit blessing of Washington.

articles.headline: Trump's Revised 10% Global Tariffs Now in Effect After Court Setback
articles.subheadline: New blanket duties represent White House effort to salvage trade agenda after Supreme Court struck down original tariff regime
articles.body:
President Donald Trump's replacement 10% tariffs on imports from virtually all trading partners took effect on Monday, marking the administration's latest attempt to reshape global trade after the Supreme Court invalidated his earlier, more aggressive tariff structure.

The new duties represent a recalibrated approach following the high court's ruling that struck down the original sweeping tariffs Trump had imposed during his trade offensive. Rather than the varied and in some cases far higher rates previously levied on individual countries, the revised regime applies a uniform 10% levy on goods entering the United States from around the world. The flattened rate structure appears designed to address legal vulnerabilities that led to the Supreme Court's intervention while still preserving the core of the president's protectionist trade philosophy.

The move carries significant implications for global supply chains, consumer prices, and diplomatic relations with key trading partners. A blanket 10% tariff, while lower than some of the targeted duties Trump originally sought, still represents a substantial barrier to trade that will ripple through industries from electronics to agriculture. Economists have warned that even a uniform rate at this level could add measurable inflationary pressure on American households and provoke retaliatory measures from major economies including the European Union and China.

For the White House, the new tariff structure is a political balancing act — demonstrating that Trump remains committed to his signature economic nationalist agenda while operating within the boundaries set by the judiciary. The Supreme Court's earlier ruling had dealt a significant blow to the administration's trade authority, raising constitutional questions about the extent of executive power over tariff policy. By moving quickly to reimpose duties at a legally defensible level, the administration is signaling it will push its trade powers to the limit of what the courts will allow.

Market participants and trade analysts will be closely watching for retaliatory actions from foreign governments and for any further legal challenges to the revised tariff regime. The durability of the new duties may ultimately depend on whether the uniform 10% rate can withstand the same judicial scrutiny that toppled its predecessor.

articles.headline: Bitcoin on Track for Worst Monthly Plunge Since 2022 Crypto Winter
articles.subheadline: The leading cryptocurrency has shed more than 19% in February, falling below $63,000 amid broad digital asset weakness
articles.body:
Bitcoin is heading for its steepest monthly decline in nearly three years, with February losses exceeding 19% and dragging the token below $63,000 — a slide not seen since the wave of corporate collapses that defined the 2022 crypto winter.

The cryptocurrency fell as much as 2.64% on Tuesday to $62,858, hovering near that level during early London trading hours, according to Bloomberg data. If February's losses hold through the end of the month, it would mark Bitcoin's worst monthly performance since June 2022, when the spectacular implosion of the TerraUSD stablecoin set off a cascade of failures across the industry. That crisis toppled crypto hedge fund Three Arrows Capital and lending platform BlockFi, among others, and wiped hundreds of billions of dollars from digital asset markets.

The comparison to that dark chapter is striking given how different the macro backdrop appeared heading into 2026. Bitcoin had benefited from the approval of spot ETFs in the United States, growing institutional adoption, and a broadly supportive political environment under the Trump administration, which has signaled interest in crypto-friendly regulation. Yet February's rout suggests those tailwinds have not been enough to insulate the market from renewed selling pressure.

Several factors appear to be weighing on sentiment. Broader risk-off moves across global markets have hit speculative assets particularly hard, and the crypto sector has faced its own headwinds, including a reported hack of World Liberty Financial, a project linked to the Trump family, and what analysts describe as crumbling core narratives around Bitcoin's role as a hedge against inflation and economic uncertainty. Bitcoin's underperformance relative to gold and equities in recent weeks has further eroded confidence among retail traders, many of whom entered the market during its post-ETF rally.

With several trading days still remaining in February, the scale of the drawdown underscores the persistent volatility that defines cryptocurrency markets even in periods of apparent institutional maturation. Bitcoin began the month trading above $78,000, meaning the decline has erased roughly $15,000 per coin in value in a matter of weeks — a sobering reminder that the asset class remains prone to sharp corrections regardless of the prevailing narrative.

articles.headline: Stanford Team Develops Nasal Spray Vaccine Shielding Against COVID, Flu, and Pneumonia
articles.subheadline: Mouse study shows a single intranasal formulation cut viral loads 700-fold and blocked bacteria and allergens for months
articles.body:
Researchers at Stanford Medicine have demonstrated an experimental nasal spray vaccine that protected mice against a sweeping range of respiratory threats — including coronaviruses, hospital-acquired bacterial infections, and common allergens — for at least three months, according to findings published February 19 in the journal Science.

The formulation, designated GLA-3M-052-LS+OVA, breaks sharply from more than two centuries of vaccine design. Rather than training the immune system to recognize a specific pathogen, it supercharges the lungs' innate defenses by mimicking the cytokine signals T cells normally exchange during active infection. A harmless egg protein, ovalbumin, is included to draw T cells into the lungs, where they sustain heightened innate immune activity for weeks to months. In vaccinated mice given three intranasal doses, SARS-CoV-2 levels in the lungs dropped 700-fold compared with unvaccinated controls. All vaccinated animals survived viral challenge, while unvaccinated mice suffered severe weight loss, widespread lung inflammation, and frequent death.

The breadth of protection surprised even the study's architects. Beyond coronaviruses, the vaccine shielded mice against Staphylococcus aureus and Acinetobacter baumannii — two bacteria responsible for dangerous hospital-acquired pneumonias — for roughly three months. When researchers exposed vaccinated animals to house dust mite proteins, a leading trigger of allergic asthma, the mice showed markedly weaker allergic responses and maintained clear airways. "I think what we have is a universal vaccine against diverse respiratory threats," said senior author Bali Pulendran, the Violetta L. Horton Professor II and professor of microbiology and immunology at Stanford. Lead author Haibo Zhang, a postdoctoral scholar in Pulendran's lab, conducted much of the experimental work.

The concept grew out of a 2023 discovery by Pulendran's group showing that the Bacillus Calmette-Guérin tuberculosis vaccine, given to roughly 100 million newborns each year, unexpectedly sustained innate immune activation for months through T-cell-derived cytokine signals acting on toll-like receptors. The new vaccine was engineered to reproduce that mechanism synthetically. "We speculated that it would be possible to make a synthetic vaccine — perhaps a nasal spray — that has the right combination of toll-like receptor stimuli," Pulendran said. "Fast forward two and a half years and we've shown that exactly what we had speculated is feasible in mice."

The team plans to move next into a Phase I human safety trial. Pulendran estimates that two nasal spray doses may suffice for people and projects that, with adequate funding, a universal respiratory vaccine could reach clinical availability within five to seven years. If results translate from mice to humans, a single autumn nasal spray could one day replace annual flu shots, updated COVID boosters, and other pathogen-specific immunizations — while also offering a rapid-response shield against novel pandemic viruses.

articles.headline: SpaceX Mega IPO Plans Reshape 2026 Listing Landscape
articles.subheadline: Elon Musk's rocket company eyes what could be the largest initial public offering in history, reshuffling the calendar for other listing hopefuls
articles.body:
SpaceX is preparing what could become the largest initial public offering in history, a move by Elon Musk that is already forcing other companies eyeing 2026 listings to rethink their timing and strategy, according to a report from Bloomberg.

The sheer scale of a SpaceX IPO — the company was valued at roughly $350 billion in recent private share sales — would dominate investor attention and absorb enormous amounts of capital, creating both logistical and competitive challenges for other firms planning to go public in the same window. Investment banks stand to collect unprecedented fees from underwriting a deal of this magnitude, making it the most coveted mandate on Wall Street.

For SpaceX's existing investors, including sovereign wealth funds, venture capital firms, and late-stage private equity backers, a public listing would provide a long-awaited liquidity event for stakes in a company that has remained private far longer than most firms of its size and influence. The company operates both its Falcon and Starship rocket programs as well as Starlink, its rapidly expanding satellite internet constellation, which alone has been valued by analysts as a potential standalone public company worth well over $100 billion.

The ripple effects across the IPO market could be significant. Companies that had been building toward 2026 debuts now face a strategic calculation: launch ahead of SpaceX to avoid competing for the same pool of institutional capital, or wait until after the offering clears to benefit from renewed investor enthusiasm for growth-stage companies. Major listings have historically set the tone for broader IPO windows, and a deal of this size could either crowd out competitors or, if successful, galvanize appetite for new issuances.

The planned offering also arrives amid a complicated backdrop for Musk personally, as his role leading the Department of Government Efficiency under the Trump administration has drawn intense public scrutiny. How investors weigh that political exposure against SpaceX's dominant market position in commercial launch services and satellite broadband will be one of the most closely watched dynamics of the 2026 capital markets calendar.

articles.headline: Researchers Demonstrate Non-Invasive Microwave Method for Deep Brain Stimulation
articles.subheadline: A new technique combining time-reversal algorithms and genetic optimization could eliminate the need for surgical implants in treating neurological disorders
articles.body:
A team of researchers has developed a proof-of-concept method for focusing microwave fields deep inside the human brain without surgery, potentially offering a non-invasive alternative to conventional deep brain stimulation (DBS) — a treatment widely used for Parkinson's disease, essential tremor, and other neurological conditions that currently requires the surgical implantation of electrodes.

The study, published on the arXiv preprint server on February 9 by Mika Söderström, Melker Carlsson, Patrik Nicolausson, and Mariana Dalarsson, introduces a technique that uses an external antenna array to deliver amplitude-modulated electromagnetic fields to precise targets within the brain. The method pairs two computational strategies: iterative time-reversal (iTR), which determines an initial antenna configuration, and a genetic-algorithm-based temporal interference (TI) optimization, which refines the positions, orientations, operating frequency, amplitudes, and phases of each antenna element to achieve sharp focal precision.

Conventional DBS has proven effective but carries significant risks inherent to brain surgery, including infection, hemorrhage, and hardware complications. Non-invasive alternatives have long been sought, but the challenge of directing electromagnetic energy to deep brain structures — while avoiding excessive stimulation of surrounding tissue — has remained formidable. The new approach addresses this by modeling magnetic point dipoles as idealized radiative sources, providing what the authors describe as a "design-independent benchmark" that is not tied to any specific antenna hardware. Crucially, the simulations incorporate realistic tissue properties drawn from voxelized anatomical head models, accounting for the complex variations in permittivity and conductivity across different brain regions, skull, and skin.

The results show that the combined iTR-TI pipeline can selectively shape amplitude-modulated fields within predefined focal regions inside a realistic head model. The iTR step provides a reliable starting configuration that accelerates convergence of the subsequent genetic algorithm, improving both speed and accuracy of the optimization. While the work remains a computational proof-of-principle rather than an experimental demonstration, the authors argue it establishes a credible foundation for future hardware development and clinical testing.

If validated experimentally, the technique could transform treatment for millions of patients with movement disorders, depression, and epilepsy who either cannot undergo surgery or face complications from implanted devices. The approach also opens broader questions about using focused microwave fields for other therapeutic and diagnostic applications in neuroscience. Further work will be needed to assess safety margins, thermal effects, and whether the focal precision achieved in simulation translates to real-world anatomical variability.

articles.headline: Nippon Steel to Raise $3.5 Billion in Convertible Bond Sale
articles.subheadline: Japanese steelmaker plans overseas issuance to refinance loans tied to its acquisition of U.S. Steel
articles.body:
Nippon Steel Corp. is preparing a 550 billion yen ($3.5 billion) convertible bond offering in overseas markets, as the Japanese steelmaker moves to restructure the financing behind its landmark acquisition of United States Steel Corp.

The bond sale represents one of the largest convertible debt issuances out of Japan in recent memory and signals Nippon Steel's confidence in its ability to tap international capital markets on favorable terms. Convertible bonds, which give holders the option to exchange debt for equity at a predetermined price, typically carry lower interest rates than conventional bonds — a potential advantage as the company seeks to reduce the cost of carrying its U.S. Steel acquisition debt.

The proceeds will be directed toward repaying loans that Nippon Steel arranged to finance the U.S. Steel takeover, a deal that drew intense political scrutiny in Washington before ultimately clearing regulatory hurdles. The acquisition, which made Nippon Steel the owner of one of America's most storied industrial names, required significant bridge financing that the company is now looking to replace with longer-duration capital market instruments.

By issuing convertible bonds overseas rather than in domestic markets, Nippon Steel is positioning itself to attract a broad base of global institutional investors, including hedge funds and long-only managers that specialize in convertible securities. The structure also limits immediate dilution to existing shareholders, since conversion would only occur if the stock appreciates above a set threshold.

The planned issuance underscores the scale of financial engineering required to digest a cross-border steel industry mega-deal. For Nippon Steel, successfully executing the bond sale would mark a critical step in placing its U.S. Steel ownership on a more sustainable financial footing, while freeing up balance sheet capacity for the operational integration challenges that lie ahead.

articles.headline: Lord Mandelson Released on Bail After Arrest on Misconduct Charge
articles.subheadline: The 72-year-old former Labour minister arrived home in London around 2:00 AM after hours of police questioning
articles.body:
Lord Peter Mandelson, the veteran Labour politician and former Cabinet minister, has been released on bail following his arrest on Monday on suspicion of misconduct in public office, the Metropolitan Police confirmed. The 72-year-old peer arrived back at his London home at approximately 02:00 GMT after spending hours in police custody.

The Metropolitan Police stated that a 72-year-old man had been released pending further investigation, though it did not name the individual directly. The arrest adds Mandelson to a growing list of prominent British public figures who have faced police scrutiny in recent months, following the high-profile arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor — formerly known as Prince Andrew — on similar misconduct in public office charges in February. Both cases appear connected to the broader fallout from the Jeffrey Epstein scandal, which has continued to reverberate through elite circles on both sides of the Atlantic.

Mandelson, a key architect of New Labour and twice a Cabinet minister under Tony Blair, has long been one of the most influential figures in British politics. He served as European Commissioner for Trade from 2004 to 2008 and was elevated to the House of Lords in 2008. His connections span the worlds of politics, business, and international diplomacy. Reports have previously noted social links between Mandelson and Epstein, the convicted sex offender who died in a New York jail cell in 2019.

The bail release means the investigation remains active, with police continuing to gather evidence before deciding whether to pursue formal charges. Misconduct in public office is a common law offence in England and Wales that carries a maximum sentence of life imprisonment, though such severe penalties are rarely imposed. The charge typically applies to public officials who willfully neglect their duty or misuse their position of trust.

Neither Lord Mandelson nor his representatives have issued a public statement regarding the arrest. The case is likely to intensify scrutiny of Epstein's network of high-profile associates in the United Kingdom, where law enforcement agencies appear to be taking an increasingly aggressive posture. Further developments are expected as the Metropolitan Police continues its investigation.

articles.headline: Oil Prices Tick Higher as Iran Nuclear Deal Uncertainty Lingers
articles.subheadline: Traders weigh Trump's mixed signals on diplomacy with Tehran, balancing deal prospects against threat of consequences
articles.body:
Crude oil prices edged higher on Monday as markets digested conflicting signals from Washington over the prospect of a renewed nuclear agreement with Iran, a development that could reshape global supply dynamics.

President Donald Trump said over the weekend that he favored a diplomatic resolution to the standoff over Iran's nuclear program but cautioned that failure to reach an accord would carry significant consequences. The remarks left traders parsing the likelihood of two starkly different outcomes — a deal that could eventually bring sanctioned Iranian barrels back onto the global market, or an escalation that could further constrain supply from one of OPEC's key producers.

The ambiguity has injected fresh volatility into a crude market already navigating a complex landscape of geopolitical risk, shifting demand forecasts, and ongoing OPEC+ production management. A successful agreement with Tehran could unlock substantial additional supply, with Iran estimated to hold the capacity to add more than one million barrels per day to global markets if sanctions were fully lifted. That prospect has historically acted as a bearish weight on prices. Conversely, a breakdown in talks — particularly one accompanied by punitive measures — could tighten an already carefully balanced market.

Energy analysts note that the market's muted upward move reflects the deep uncertainty surrounding the negotiations rather than a decisive directional bet. Traders appear reluctant to price in either outcome with conviction, opting instead for cautious positioning as diplomatic developments unfold. The situation adds another variable to a first quarter already shaped by questions about Chinese demand recovery, U.S. production levels, and the timeline for any adjustments to OPEC+ output quotas.

For now, the oil market remains in a holding pattern, with geopolitical risk premiums fluctuating in step with the latest diplomatic rhetoric. Further clarity on the trajectory of U.S.-Iran talks — or the lack thereof — is likely to be the dominant catalyst for crude in the near term.

articles.headline: Chinese Stocks Rally After Lunar New Year on Tariff Easing and AI Optimism
articles.subheadline: Onshore markets reopen higher as traders weigh US trade relief and domestic tech momentum against uneven consumer data
articles.body:
Chinese equities climbed as mainland markets reopened following a nine-day Lunar New Year holiday, buoyed by a confluence of easing trade tensions with the United States and growing enthusiasm around China's domestic artificial intelligence sector.

The rally reflected a shift in sentiment during the extended break, as developments on two key fronts gave investors reason for optimism. On trade, signals that Washington may pursue a less aggressive tariff posture toward Beijing helped alleviate one of the market's most persistent headwinds. Meanwhile, China's rapidly maturing AI ecosystem — highlighted by a string of announcements from domestic technology firms over the holiday period — continued to energize a sector that has become central to the country's economic narrative.

The gains came despite a mixed picture on consumer spending during the Lunar New Year holiday, traditionally one of the strongest periods for domestic consumption. While travel and tourism figures showed resilience, broader retail and spending data painted an uneven picture, underscoring the ongoing challenges facing China's consumer recovery. The tension between robust technology-sector momentum and a still-fragile demand environment has defined Chinese markets in recent months.

For global investors, the reopening move carries significance beyond a single session. Chinese equities have been volatile in recent years, whipsawed by regulatory crackdowns, property sector stress, and geopolitical friction. The current rally suggests that the combination of trade de-escalation and a credible domestic growth story in AI and advanced technology may be sufficient to sustain renewed interest. Analysts have noted that China's progress in large language models, semiconductor design, and autonomous systems has begun to rival developments in Silicon Valley, providing a fundamental underpinning for tech-heavy indices.

The market's trajectory in the coming weeks will likely depend on whether the tariff relief materializes into concrete policy changes and whether corporate earnings from China's technology champions can match the elevated expectations now priced into shares. With Beijing continuing to signal support for its technology sector as a pillar of economic strategy, traders appear willing — for now — to look past the consumer spending headwinds and bet on innovation-led growth.

articles.headline: IBM Shares Suffer Worst Day Since 2000 After Anthropic's COBOL Tool Announcement
articles.subheadline: Anthropic's Claude Code threatens IBM's lucrative legacy mainframe business by promising to modernize decades-old COBOL systems
articles.body:
Shares of International Business Machines Corp. plunged on Monday in the company's steepest single-day decline in more than a quarter century, triggered by an announcement from artificial intelligence startup Anthropic that its Claude Code tool can assist in modernizing COBOL — the decades-old programming language that remains a cornerstone of IBM's mainframe business.

The selloff reflects deep investor anxiety over what has long been considered one of IBM's most durable competitive advantages: the sheer difficulty of migrating legacy COBOL systems to modern platforms. Tens of billions of lines of COBOL code still underpin critical infrastructure across banking, insurance, government, and healthcare, much of it running on IBM mainframes that generate substantial recurring revenue through hardware, software licensing, and maintenance contracts. The prospect that AI could dramatically lower the cost and complexity of modernizing those systems strikes at the heart of IBM's business model.

Anthropic, the San Francisco-based AI company behind the Claude family of large language models, said its Claude Code tool can help organizations translate and refactor COBOL codebases — a task that has historically required scarce specialized programmers and years-long migration projects costing hundreds of millions of dollars. If the technology delivers on its promise, enterprises that have been locked into IBM's mainframe ecosystem for decades could find a viable off-ramp far sooner than previously anticipated.

The magnitude of Monday's drop — the worst since 2000 — underscores how central the legacy moat has been to IBM's investment thesis. While the company has spent years pivoting toward hybrid cloud and AI under CEO Arvind Krishna, its mainframe division continues to anchor profitability. Analysts have long viewed the stickiness of COBOL-dependent clients as a stabilizing force, making the Anthropic announcement a direct challenge to assumptions baked into IBM's valuation.

The development also highlights a broader dynamic playing out across the technology industry: generative AI tools are increasingly capable of automating complex software engineering tasks that once required deep domain expertise. For IBM, the strategic question now is whether its own AI investments — including its Watsonx platform — can evolve quickly enough to keep enterprise clients within its orbit, even as rivals offer tools designed to liberate them from it.

articles.headline: Trump Reinstates 10% Global Tariff Under Trade Act Authority
articles.subheadline: New baseline levy takes effect for 150 days after Supreme Court struck down earlier, broader tariff regime
articles.body:
President Donald Trump has imposed a new 10% baseline tariff on imports from around the world, invoking authority under the 1974 Trade Act after the Supreme Court invalidated his earlier and more sweeping levy regime. The tariff, which takes effect immediately, will remain in place for 150 days.

The White House has argued that the president can impose the new tariff without congressional approval, citing provisions in the decades-old trade legislation that grant the executive branch significant latitude to address trade imbalances. The move represents a recalibration of Trump's trade strategy after the judiciary dealt a significant blow to his initial tariff architecture, which had imposed sharply higher duties on a country-by-country basis.

The 10% universal rate is considerably more modest than the differentiated tariffs Trump had previously enacted, which in some cases exceeded 100% on imports from specific nations. However, the flat-rate approach still marks a substantial shift in U.S. trade policy, effectively raising the cost of imported goods across the board for American businesses and consumers. The 150-day window suggests the administration views the measure as a bridge — potentially toward negotiated trade deals or a more durable tariff framework that could survive judicial scrutiny.

Markets and trading partners will be closely watching whether the legal basis for the new tariff holds up to the inevitable challenges. The 1974 Trade Act has historically provided presidents with broad but not unlimited authority to impose trade restrictions, and legal scholars remain divided on whether a blanket global tariff fits within its scope. The Supreme Court's earlier ruling demonstrated the judiciary's willingness to check executive overreach on trade, raising the stakes for this latest maneuver.

The tariff's implementation adds another layer of uncertainty to global supply chains already strained by years of trade disputes. For multinational corporations, the 10% levy — while lower than feared — still reshapes cost calculations across virtually every import category. Trading partners from Europe to Asia are expected to weigh retaliatory measures, even as some may see the reduced rate as an opening for bilateral negotiations during the 150-day window.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Slides to $64,000 as AI Disruption Fears Hammer IBM, Broader Markets
articles.subheadline: Anthropic's COBOL automation announcement triggers 11% IBM plunge, dragging crypto and equities sharply lower
articles.body:
Bitcoin tumbled 5% to $64,000 on Monday as a fresh wave of AI-driven disruption fears rippled through global markets, with IBM plunging 11% after Anthropic announced its Claude platform can automate the modernization of COBOL — the decades-old programming language that underpins much of the world's financial infrastructure and has long been a lucrative consulting revenue stream for the tech giant.

Anthropic said COBOL handles an estimated 95% of ATM transactions in the United States, with hundreds of billions of lines of the language running in production daily across finance, airlines, and government systems. The company noted that the engineers who originally built these systems have largely retired, and universities barely teach the language anymore. Modernizing COBOL systems "once required armies of consultants spending years mapping workflows," Anthropic said — a description that maps squarely onto IBM's business model. The announcement sent IBM shares down 11.2% near the close, dragging the Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq each lower by more than 1%.

Cryptocurrencies, which have traded in tight correlation with software stocks in recent months, bore the brunt of the risk-off mood. Ether and Solana fell by similar margins to bitcoin, while crypto-adjacent equities including Coinbase, Strategy, Circle, and Galaxy Digital declined between 4% and 7%. Several major tokens are now approaching their lows from the February 5 panic selloff, with bitcoin subsequently dipping below $63,000 in after-hours trading. Bitcoin ETFs have now recorded $3.8 billion in outflows over a historic five-week streak, underscoring waning institutional appetite.

The notable exceptions to the carnage were bitcoin miners that have pivoted toward AI infrastructure. IREN rose 5%, Cipher Mining gained 3.4%, CleanSpark added 1.5%, and Hut 8 climbed 0.7% — a divergence that highlights how AI disruption is simultaneously destroying and creating value across the digital asset ecosystem. Precious metals also surged as safe-haven assets, with gold jumping 3.2% to $5,243 per ounce and silver rallying 6.5% to $87.69.

Analysts are watching the $60,000 level as critical support for bitcoin, warning that a breach could open a path toward the mid-to-low $50,000 range. "It's becoming increasingly clear how pivotal the times we are in right now truly are," wrote The Kobeissi Letter, capturing the growing anxiety that AI advances are systematically dismantling established business models faster than markets can price in the disruption. The pattern of AI announcements triggering broad selloffs — now hitting legacy tech, software, and crypto in rapid succession — shows no signs of abating.

articles.headline: New AI Foundation Model Bridges Quantum Accuracy and Biological Scale
articles.subheadline: UBio-MolFM achieves quantum-level precision for biomolecular simulations of up to 1,500 atoms, a major leap for computational biology
articles.body:
A team of researchers has unveiled UBio-MolFM, a universal molecular foundation model designed to solve one of computational biology's most persistent challenges: simulating large biological systems with quantum-mechanical accuracy without prohibitive computational cost.

The work, posted to the arXiv preprint server on February 13 by Lin Huang, Jia Zhang, and nine collaborators, tackles the fundamental trade-off that has long constrained molecular simulation. Quantum-mechanical methods deliver high accuracy but cannot scale to the thousands of atoms found in real biological environments, while cheaper classical approximations sacrifice the precision needed to capture subtle biochemical phenomena. UBio-MolFM aims to deliver both simultaneously through three interlocking technical advances.

At the model's core is UBio-Mol26, a large bio-specific dataset built using what the authors call a "Two-Pronged Strategy" — combining systematic bottom-up enumeration of molecular fragments with top-down sampling from native protein environments encompassing up to 1,200 atoms. The architecture itself, dubbed E2Former-V2, is a linear-scaling equivariant transformer that employs novel sparsification and long-short range modeling techniques to capture non-local physical interactions. The team reports up to roughly four times higher inference throughput on large-system benchmarks compared to existing approaches. A three-stage curriculum learning protocol rounds out the framework, progressively training the model from energy initialization through energy-force consistency and finally force-focused supervision.

The researchers validated UBio-MolFM across both microscopic and macroscopic observables, testing it on liquid water structure, ionic solvation, and peptide folding — problems that span vastly different length scales and physical regimes. According to the paper, the model achieves ab initio-level fidelity on biomolecular systems containing up to approximately 1,500 atoms, including out-of-distribution configurations not seen during training. This generalization capability is particularly significant, as it suggests the model could serve as a practical tool rather than a narrow benchmark performer.

The implications for drug discovery, protein engineering, and fundamental biological research could be substantial. Traditional all-atom molecular dynamics simulations — sometimes described as a "computational microscope" — have been limited to either small systems at quantum accuracy or large systems at reduced fidelity. By reconciling scalability with quantum precision, UBio-MolFM represents a potential step change in what computational biologists can reliably simulate, positioning foundation models as increasingly central infrastructure in the life sciences.

articles.headline: Bouncing Universe Theory Offers Unified Origin for Black Holes and Dark Matter
articles.subheadline: New model proposes that cosmic relics from a pre-Big Bang contraction phase could explain several open puzzles in cosmology
articles.body:
A theoretical framework accepted for publication in Physical Review D proposes that black holes, gravitational waves, and dark matter may all trace their origins to a single mechanism: the collapse and rebound of a bouncing universe that preceded the current era of cosmic expansion.

Enrique Gaztanaga, in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server, outlines a cosmological model in which the universe underwent a contraction phase before bouncing into the expansion observed today. During that pre-bounce collapse, the paper argues, two distinct channels produced lasting relics. In the first, compact objects and gravitational waves formed during contraction grew larger than the particle horizon — the maximum distance light could have traveled — and survived the bounce intact, later re-entering the observable universe. In the second, dark matter halos that formed during collapse exited the horizon and subsequently collapsed into black holes upon re-entry. Gaztanaga derives specific horizon-crossing conditions and finds that perturbations or compact objects larger than approximately 90 meters would persist through the bounce event.

The proposal marks a significant departure from the standard inflationary paradigm, which posits that primordial black holes emerged from quantum fluctuations stretched to macroscopic scales during a brief period of exponential expansion. In Gaztanaga's bouncing cosmology, relic black holes instead arise from nonlinear structure formation — the gravitational clumping of matter during the contraction phase — a process that is physically distinct and potentially more robust than the fine-tuned conditions typically required in inflationary models.

The resulting population of relic black holes and gravitational waves spans a wide range of masses, which the author argues could simultaneously address several persistent cosmological puzzles: the nature of dark matter, the origin of the stochastic gravitational-wave background that pulsar timing arrays have recently begun to detect, and the unexpectedly rapid growth of supermassive black holes and galaxies in the early universe — a phenomenon that has puzzled astronomers analyzing data from the James Webb Space Telescope.

While bouncing cosmologies remain a minority position in theoretical physics compared to inflationary models, they have attracted renewed interest as observational tensions accumulate. The paper's acceptance by Physical Review D lends it a measure of peer-reviewed credibility, though the framework will require testable predictions that can be distinguished from competing theories. If the model's signatures — particularly its predictions for gravitational-wave spectra and primordial black hole mass distributions — prove consistent with upcoming data from gravitational-wave observatories and large-scale structure surveys, it could reshape understanding of the universe's earliest moments.

articles.headline: Lord Mandelson Led Away by Police From London Home Amid Epstein Probe
articles.subheadline: Former UK ambassador to the US escorted by plain-clothes officers as investigation into alleged sharing of market-sensitive information continues
articles.body:
Lord Peter Mandelson was led away from his north London residence by police on Monday afternoon, in a dramatic escalation of the investigation into his alleged connections to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Plain-clothes officers escorted the 72-year-old from his Camden home and placed him in the back of an unmarked vehicle, according to footage broadcast by the BBC. The former Labour cabinet minister and ex-British ambassador to the United States has been under investigation over allegations that he shared market-sensitive government information with Epstein during his time as a minister. The nature and scope of the information allegedly disclosed have not been publicly detailed by authorities.

Mandelson, one of the architects of New Labour and a close ally of former Prime Minister Tony Blair, has not commented publicly in recent weeks on the Epstein files. However, the BBC reported that his consistent position has been that he did not act criminally and was not motivated by financial gain. It remains unclear whether Monday's police action constitutes a formal arrest or another step in the ongoing inquiry. Neither the Metropolitan Police nor Mandelson's representatives have issued official statements clarifying the situation.

The development marks a significant moment in the broader fallout from the Epstein scandal, which has ensnared prominent figures across politics, business, and entertainment on both sides of the Atlantic. Epstein, a financier convicted of sex trafficking, died in a New York jail cell in August 2019 in what was ruled a suicide. The subsequent release of court documents and flight logs has drawn scrutiny to numerous high-profile individuals who had contact with Epstein, though association alone does not imply wrongdoing.

Mandelson's political career has been defined by both influence and controversy. He served twice in the Cabinet under Blair, resigning on both occasions, before later being elevated to the House of Lords and appointed European Commissioner for Trade. Most recently, he served as Britain's ambassador to Washington before being replaced earlier this year. His detention is likely to send shockwaves through Westminster and raises fresh questions about the reach of the Epstein investigation into the upper echelons of British political life.

articles.headline: Dimon Warns of Pre-2008 Parallels as Rivals Do 'Dumb Things'
articles.subheadline: JPMorgan CEO sees echoes of reckless lending era as financial industry competition intensifies
articles.body:
JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon sounded an alarm at an investor event on Monday, telling attendees that the current competitive landscape in financial services is beginning to resemble the reckless environment that preceded the 2008 global financial crisis. The blunt-spoken banking chief said rivals are engaging in "dumb things" as they chase market share across lending and other business lines.

Dimon's warning carries particular weight given his tenure atop the largest U.S. bank by assets. He steered JPMorgan through the 2008 meltdown in stronger shape than virtually any major competitor, and his willingness to draw explicit comparisons to that era suggests genuine concern about risk standards across the industry. In the years leading up to the crisis, banks dramatically loosened underwriting standards in a frenzy to originate mortgages and other loans, a race to the bottom that ultimately triggered a near-collapse of the global financial system.

The comments come at a moment when U.S. banks are competing aggressively for loan growth amid a relatively stable economy. Private credit firms, fintech lenders, and traditional banks are all vying for borrowers, creating pressure to offer more favorable terms and accept thinner margins. Dimon did not single out specific institutions or lending categories, but his remarks imply that some competitors are sacrificing credit discipline in pursuit of volume — a dynamic that historically precedes spikes in loan losses.

Dimon also addressed JPMorgan's approach to artificial intelligence during the event, signaling that the bank views the technology as a transformative force it must navigate carefully. JPMorgan has been among the most aggressive large banks in deploying AI across trading, risk management, and customer-facing operations, investing billions in technology infrastructure in recent years.

The warning from Wall Street's most prominent CEO is likely to draw scrutiny from regulators and investors alike. While Dimon has long been known for cautionary rhetoric — frequently cataloging geopolitical and economic risks in his annual shareholder letters — a direct comparison to the pre-crisis period represents an escalation in tone. Whether the industry heeds the warning or dismisses it as competitive posturing may only become clear when the credit cycle eventually turns.

articles.headline: First UK Baby Born From Deceased-Donor Womb Transplant
articles.subheadline: Ten-week-old Hugo marks a milestone in a clinical trial that could offer hope to thousands of women born without a viable uterus
articles.body:
A baby boy named Hugo has become the first child in the United Kingdom born using a womb transplanted from a deceased donor, in what surgeons are calling a ground-breaking moment for organ transplantation and reproductive medicine.

Hugo was born just before Christmas 2024 at Queen Charlotte's and Chelsea Hospital in west London, weighing nearly seven pounds. His mother, Grace Bell, a woman in her 30s from Kent, was born with MRKH syndrome — a condition affecting roughly 5,000 women in the UK that results in the absence of a viable uterus. Bell was told at age 16 that she would never be able to carry her own child. She received the transplanted womb in a 10-hour operation at The Churchill Hospital in Oxford in June 2024, followed by IVF treatment and embryo transfer at The Lister Fertility Clinic in London.

Bell and her partner Steve Powell described Hugo as "simply a miracle" and paid tribute to the anonymous donor and her family for their "incredible gift." The donor's parents said they felt "tremendous pride" in their daughter's legacy; in addition to her womb, she donated five other organs that were transplanted into four recipients. As a gesture of gratitude, Bell and Powell gave Hugo the middle name Richard, after consultant gynaecologist Prof. Richard Smith of Imperial College Healthcare NHS Trust, who has researched womb transplantation for more than 25 years and was present at the birth.

The successful delivery is the first from the deceased-donor arm of a UK clinical research trial that plans 10 such transplants; three have been completed so far. A separate track of the trial involves living donors — earlier in 2025, a baby named Amy became the first UK child born from a living womb donation, after her mother received a uterus from her older sister. Five additional living-donor transplants are planned. Globally, more than 100 womb transplant operations have been performed, resulting in over 70 healthy births, though very few in Europe have involved deceased donors.

Transplant surgeon Isabel Quiroga, who co-leads the trial, said the team is investigating whether the procedure can become an approved, routine treatment for women of childbearing age who lack a viable womb. The transplanted organ is designed to be temporary: after Bell decides whether to have a second child, surgeons will remove the womb to spare her from a lifetime of immunosuppressive drugs. The birth nonetheless represents a significant expansion of reproductive options for women with MRKH syndrome and similar conditions, offering what Prof. Smith described as genuine hope where none previously existed.

articles.headline: Jane Street Sued for Insider Trading by Terraform Labs Administrator
articles.subheadline: The administrator winding down the collapsed crypto firm alleges the trading giant engaged in insider trading tied to Terra's $40 billion implosion
articles.body:
Jane Street Group, one of the world's most prominent quantitative trading firms, is facing an insider trading lawsuit filed by the administrator overseeing the wind-down of Terraform Labs, the crypto company whose spectacular $40 billion collapse in 2022 sent shockwaves through digital asset markets.

The lawsuit, details of which emerged on Monday, alleges that Jane Street engaged in insider trading connected to Terraform's operations. Terraform Labs, founded by Do Kwon, imploded in May 2022 when its algorithmic stablecoin TerraUSD (UST) and sister token Luna lost virtually all of their value in a matter of days. The collapse wiped out tens of billions of dollars in investor wealth and is widely credited with triggering a cascading crisis across the crypto industry — one that ultimately contributed to the downfall of Sam Bankman-Fried's FTX exchange later that year.

Jane Street, a New York-based trading firm known for its dominance in options market-making and its growing presence in crypto markets, has not yet publicly commented on the allegations. The firm, which reported more than $20 billion in net trading revenue in recent years, has been one of the most active institutional participants in digital asset trading. Its involvement with Terraform-related tokens during the period surrounding the collapse now faces legal scrutiny from those tasked with recovering assets for creditors.

The suit adds another layer to the sprawling legal aftermath of the Terra collapse. Do Kwon was extradited to the United States in 2024 and faces criminal fraud charges. Terraform Labs itself reached a $4.47 billion settlement with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission in June 2024 after a jury found the company liable for securities fraud. The administrator managing Terraform's remaining affairs is now apparently seeking to claw back funds from trading counterparties it believes profited improperly.

The case underscores the continuing legal fallout from crypto's 2022 crisis and raises questions about the trading practices of major institutional players during one of the most turbulent episodes in digital asset history. For Jane Street, which has cultivated a reputation as a sophisticated and disciplined trading operation, the allegations represent a rare and significant legal challenge.

articles.headline: Mexico Reveals Operation That Took Down Nation's Most-Wanted Cartel Leader
articles.subheadline: Government emphasizes operation was conducted without active U.S. participation, marking Sheinbaum's boldest move against cartels
articles.body:
Mexico's government has disclosed details of the operation that led to the capture of the country's most-wanted drug cartel chief, in what represents President Claudia Sheinbaum's most significant direct confrontation with organized crime since taking office.

In a detailed public accounting, Mexican authorities emphasized that U.S. security agents did not actively participate in the operation — a politically significant distinction for Sheinbaum's administration, which has sought to assert Mexican sovereignty over domestic security matters even as Washington has pressed for deeper cooperation on drug enforcement. The insistence on operational independence reflects the broader diplomatic tension between the two countries over cartel strategy and cross-border drug trafficking.
